arg0.description: A cookie header string containing multiple cookie definitions with the same name but different attributes (e.g., different $Path values) that causes the CookieDecoder to incorrectly parse cookie attributes, leading to integer overflow in getMaxAge() returning Integer.MIN_VALUE. The payload must contain duplicate cookie names with conflicting attribute assignments that trigger parsing inconsistencies in the decoder.

ground_truth_payload.description: This is the entry layer for the vulnerability chain. The method cookieValue() calls cookie() with the provided name parameter. According to the sink-level constraints, we need to provide a cookie name that exists in the parsed cookie list. The cookie list is derived from parsing the Cookie header in this.headers. The sink requirement requires a cookie header string containing duplicate cookie names with conflicting attributes that causes parsing inconsistencies. Therefore, we need to set up the headers field with a Cookie header containing the malicious payload, and provide a cookie name that matches one of the duplicate names in that header. The cookie name 'session' is chosen as it's a common cookie name that would appear in the malicious header string.

arg0.description: A cookie header string containing multiple cookie definitions with the same name but different attributes (e.g., different $Path values) that causes the CookieDecoder to incorrectly parse cookie attributes, leading to integer overflow in getMaxAge() returning Integer.MIN_VALUE. The payload must contain duplicate cookie names with conflicting attribute assignments that trigger parsing inconsistencies in the decoder.

ground_truth_payload.description: This entry payload triggers the vulnerability by providing a cookie header string with duplicate cookie names containing conflicting $Path attributes. The HttpRequestWrapper.cookieValue method delegates to StubHttpRequest.cookieValue, which calls cookie(name) to retrieve the HttpCookie. The duplicate cookie names with different $Path values cause parsing inconsistencies in the CookieDecoder, leading to integer overflow in getMaxAge() returning Integer.MIN_VALUE. The concrete cookie header string contains two 'session' cookies with different $Path attributes to trigger the vulnerability.

The charset parameter must be a non-null String representing a valid character encoding

The HttpContext parameter must be null or an object that doesn't affect the execution path to determineTarget

The caller method org.apache.http.impl.client.CloseableHttpClient.determineTarget must receive an HttpUriRequest object that returns a URI matching the sink-level constraint when its getURI() method is called

arg0.description: A URI where the user-info component contains '@' characters that can be misinterpreted as host-port separators, causing URIUtils.extractHost() to incorrectly parse the authority section and potentially return null or incorrect host information. The vulnerability involves ambiguous parsing of authority components when '@' symbols appear in user credentials.

ground_truth_payload.description: This entry payload triggers the vulnerability by providing an HttpUriRequest with a URI that contains '@' characters in the user-info component. When request.getURI() is called, it returns a URI like 'http://user@pass@example.com', where the '@' in 'user@pass' can be misinterpreted as a host-port separator during parsing in downstream layers. The httpClient field is initialized to a DefaultHttpClient instance, logger is initialized to a Logger instance, context is null to avoid affecting execution, and charset is 'UTF-8' to ensure valid encoding. The execution path proceeds normally through httpClient.execute() and reaches the next layer, where the URI's ambiguous authority parsing becomes exploitable.

The field 'folder' must be a List<String> containing exactly one element
The first element of 'folder' must be the exact string that triggers the vulnerability in FilenameUtils.normalize
The caller method FileUtils.resolveToFile must receive a string argument that will be passed to FilenameUtils.normalize
The string argument must be exactly the same as the sink argument that triggers the vulnerability in FilenameUtils.normalize

arg0.description: A string containing Windows UNC path prefix (\\ followed by a hostname or IP) combined with directory traversal sequences (..\) that bypass normalization checks, causing the method to return null instead of properly resolving the path, potentially leading to path traversal vulnerabilities when the null return value is not properly handled.

ground_truth_payload.description: The InstallTemplate.run() method is the entry point that calls execute(). To trigger the vulnerability, we need to set the 'folder' field to contain exactly one element with a Windows UNC path containing directory traversal sequences. Specifically, we need a string like '\\192.168.1.100\share\..\..\evil' that will cause FilenameUtils.normalize to return null when processed in deeper layers. This null return value bypasses path normalization checks, potentially leading to path traversal vulnerabilities. The run() method itself has no parameters, so we only need to set the field value.

arg0.description: A cookie header string containing multiple cookie definitions with the same name but different attributes (e.g., different $Path values) that causes the CookieDecoder to incorrectly parse cookie attributes, leading to integer overflow in getMaxAge() returning Integer.MIN_VALUE. The payload must contain duplicate cookie names with conflicting attribute assignments that trigger parsing inconsistencies in the decoder.

ground_truth_payload.description: This is the entry layer (Layer 0) where we need to create an HttpRequestWrapper instance that delegates to a StubHttpRequest. The vulnerability requires a specific cookie header string that causes parsing inconsistencies in InboundCookieParser. According to the sink-level constraints, we need a cookie header string containing duplicate cookie names with conflicting attribute assignments. The StubHttpRequest's cookies() method calls InboundCookieParser.parse(headers(COOKIE_HEADER)), so we need to set up the StubHttpRequest's headers field with a 'Cookie' header containing the malicious payload. The HttpRequestWrapper simply delegates to this.request, so we need to instantiate it with a properly configured StubHttpRequest.

arg0.description: A cookie header string containing multiple cookie definitions with the same name but different attributes (e.g., different $Path values) that causes the CookieDecoder to incorrectly parse cookie attributes, leading to integer overflow in getMaxAge() returning Integer.MIN_VALUE. The payload must contain duplicate cookie names with conflicting attribute assignments that trigger parsing inconsistencies in the decoder.

ground_truth_payload.description: This entry payload triggers the vulnerability by calling cookieValue() with a cookie name that exists in the malicious cookie header string. The cookie header contains duplicate cookie names with conflicting $Path attributes, which will cause CookieDecoder to incorrectly parse cookie attributes. When cookie(name) is called, it will find the first matching cookie in the list, but the parsing inconsistencies in the underlying CookieDecoder will lead to integer overflow in getMaxAge() returning Integer.MIN_VALUE. The concrete value 'sessionId' is chosen as it's a common cookie name that would be present in the malicious header string.

arg0.description: A cookie header string containing multiple cookie definitions with the same name but different attributes (e.g., different $Path values) that causes the CookieDecoder to incorrectly parse cookie attributes, leading to integer overflow in getMaxAge() returning Integer.MIN_VALUE. The payload must contain duplicate cookie names with conflicting attribute assignments that trigger parsing inconsistencies in the decoder.

ground_truth_payload.description: This is the entry layer of the vulnerability chain. The HttpRequestWrapper.cookieValue() method simply delegates to the underlying NettyHttpRequest.cookieValue() method. To trigger the vulnerability, we need to pass a cookie name that exists in the malicious cookie header string. The cookie header contains duplicate cookie names with conflicting attributes that cause parsing inconsistencies in CookieDecoder. The vulnerability requires that the cookie name matches one of the duplicate names in the header, which will cause the CookieDecoder to incorrectly parse attributes and eventually lead to integer overflow in getMaxAge() returning Integer.MIN_VALUE. Since this is the entry point, we need to provide the cookie name that will be looked up in the malicious cookie header.
